_comment: Hand-curated demo answers — short-circuits the LLM when a question matches all listed keywords. Tone: warm, casual, friend-explaining-to-friend.

en.answer: Pick literally any tiny window around L — even a hairline thin one. There's always a matching delta-window around a so that anything you put in, ends up inside that yellow strip. The two bands shrink together. That's the whole game.
Because the curve doesn't jump near a — it changes smoothly. So the closer x gets to a, the more the function is forced to hug L. The epsilon-delta game just makes 'closer and closer' precise.

ar.answer: اختار أي شباك صغير حوالين L، حتى لو رفيع جدًا. هتلاقي دلتا حوالين a أي قيمة x جوّاها هتدخل الشباك ده. الاتنين بيرفعوا مع بعض. ده معنى "بتقرّب من L".

en.answer: Two points on the curve, h apart. As h shrinks, those points slide together and the line through them rotates — at the moment they touch, you've got the tangent. Its slope is $f'(a)$.

You can't actually plug in $h=0$ — that's a divide-by-zero. But you can ask what the slope's heading toward as h gets tiny. That limit is the derivative — the slope of the line that just kisses the curve at a.

ar.answer: نقطتين على المنحنى، بينهم h. لما h يصغّر، النقطتين بيتلاقوا والخط اللي بينهم بيلفّ. ساعة ما يتلامسوا، بقى عندك المماس. ميله هو $f'(a)$.

en.answer: $a$ is basically the growth speed. Positive $a$ means the curve takes off — bigger $a$, faster takeoff. Negative $a$, it dies off toward zero. Zero $a$, it just sits at 1. One slider, infinite curves.

Each function stretches its input by its own derivative. The inner one stretches by $g'(x)$, then the outer one stretches that result by $f'(g(x))$. Two stretches in a row = multiply. That's literally the chain rule.

Because multiplying two unit complex numbers adds their angles — that's just how the unit circle works. So squaring doubles the angle, cubing triples it, $n$-th power multiplies by $n$. The wave on screen is $\cos(n\theta)$ — the real part of $e^{in\theta}$ — and it crams more cycles in as $n$ grows.

ar.answer: لأن ضرب عددين مركّبين على الدايرة الواحدة بيجمع الزاويتين بتوعهم — دي طبيعة الدايرة. فالتربيع بيضاعف الزاوية، التكعيب بيضربها في 3، الأُس n بيضربها في n. الموجة اللي قدامك هي cos(nθ)، وكل ما n يكبر بتلاقيها بتعمل دورات أكتر في نفس المسافة.

en.answer: Each prism is a tiny box: its base is one cell of the grid (area $\Delta x\,\Delta y$), and its height is $f(x_i, y_j)$ at some point inside the cell. So one prism's volume is $f(x_i, y_j)\,\Delta x\,\Delta y$ — the formula sums those over every cell.

As $\Delta \to 0$ the prisms get thinner and more numerous, hugging the surface from below and above more tightly. The total volume locks onto a single number — that's the double integral. No matter how you pick the sample point inside each cell, you get the same answer in the limit (that's what 'integrable' means).
It's the volume trapped between the surface $z = f(x,y)$ and the region $R$ in the xy-plane. We approximate it by stacking prisms over a grid; the limit as the grid shrinks is the integral itself.

ar.answer: كل منشور صندوق صغير: قاعدته خلية من الشبكة (مساحتها $\Delta x\,\Delta y$)، وارتفاعه $f(x_i, y_j)$ عند نقطة جوّاها. يعني حجم المنشور الواحد $f(x_i, y_j)\,\Delta x\,\Delta y$ — والصيغة بتجمع ده على كل الخلايا.
كل ما $\Delta$ تصغّر، المناشير بتبقى أرفع وأكتر، وبتقرّب من السطح من فوق ومن تحت. المجموع بيقفل على رقم واحد — ده هو التكامل المزدوج. مش مهم تختار نقطة العيّنة جوّه الخلية فين، النهاية واحدة.

en.answer: Because $e^{-x^2}$ has no elementary antiderivative — there's no closed-form $F(x)$ to plug into the fundamental theorem. Squaring turns the 1D problem into a 2D one over the whole plane, where polar coordinates make it solvable. It's a classic 'leave the building you're stuck in' move.

In Cartesian the integrand $e^{-(x^2+y^2)}$ has no nice antiderivative, but in polar coordinates the area element is $r\,dr\,d\theta$ — that extra $r$ is exactly what's needed to make $r e^{-r^2}$ an easy integral (its antiderivative is $-\tfrac{1}{2} e^{-r^2}$).

We compute $I^2 = \pi$, not $I$ directly. So $I$ is whatever positive number squares to $\pi$ — that's $\sqrt{\pi}$. The square root is the price of the squaring trick we used to get the integral solvable in the first place.

When you change variables from $(x,y)$ to $(r,\theta)$, the area element transforms by the Jacobian determinant: $dx\,dy = r\,dr\,d\theta$. That extra $r$ is the geometric stretch factor — a thin polar wedge gets wider as $r$ grows. Without it, you'd get the wrong area.

ar.answer: لأن $e^{-x^2}$ مفيش له مشتقة عكسية بالشكل المعتاد — مفيش $F(x)$ نقدر نعوّض فيها. التربيع بيحوّل المسألة من بُعد واحد لاتنين على المستوي كله، وهناك الإحداثيات القطبية بتحلّها. حركة كلاسيكية: لو علقت في مسألة، خرّجها لفضاء أكبر.
في الإحداثيات الديكارتية $e^{-(x^2+y^2)}$ صعب، بس في القطبية عنصر المساحة بيبقى $r\,dr\,d\theta$ — الـ $r$ الزيادة دي بالظبط اللي بتخلّي $r e^{-r^2}$ سهل (مشتقتها العكسية $-\tfrac{1}{2} e^{-r^2}$).

en.answer: A saddle point is a critical point that's a min in one direction and a max in another — like the seat of a horse saddle. For $z = ax^2 + by^2$ that happens whenever $a$ and $b$ have opposite signs. Spin the surface in 3D and you'll see it goes up along one axis and down along the other.

A bowl appears when both $a$ and $b$ are positive — the surface curves up no matter which way you walk away from the origin, so $(0, 0)$ is a local minimum. Flip both signs and you get an upside-down dome (a maximum).

The gradient $\nabla z = (2ax, 2by)$ vanishes only at the origin, so $(0, 0)$ is the lone critical point. The Hessian's signs (which match $a$ and $b$) classify it: both positive → minimum, both negative → maximum, opposite signs → saddle.

ar.answer: نقطة السرج هي نقطة حرجة شكلها زي سرج الحصان: تطلع في اتجاه وتنزل في الاتجاه التاني. في $z = a x^2 + b y^2$ ده بيحصل لما a و b يبقوا إشارتهم مختلفة. لفّ السطح في الـ 3D وانت هتشوف بنفسك.

en.answer: Take the two columns of the matrix as vectors and draw the parallelogram they span. The determinant is the (signed) area of that parallelogram. Negative means the matrix flipped orientation; zero means the parallelogram collapsed to a line.

When $\det = 0$, the two columns are parallel — they no longer span a 2D parallelogram, just a line. The matrix has squashed the plane onto a line, and you can't undo that, which is why a zero-determinant matrix isn't invertible.

ar.answer: خد عمودين المصفوفة كأنهم متجهين وارسم متوازي الأضلاع اللي بينهم. المحدد هو مساحة متوازي الأضلاع ده (بالإشارة). لو سالب يبقى المصفوفة قلبت الاتجاه، ولو صفر يبقى المتوازي وقع على خط واحد.
